definition: A book of hours is a lay devotional day-book: a collection of prayers, psalms, and offices arranged to be recited at the canonical hours of the day. Unlike a monk's breviary, it was made for private devotion by ordinary people, and its calendar structure made it the household prayer book of the later Middle Ages.
history: The name renders Latin *horae*, 'hours' (ultimately Greek *hōra*, 'season, time of day'), the fixed intervals at which the Church appointed prayer. The turn is one of sheer numbers: the book of hours was the runaway bestseller of the Middle Ages, produced in vast quantity and, at its finest, the chief canvas for the illuminator's art. It brought the daily rhythm of monastic prayer into the layperson's hands, and made the private book itself an instrument of piety.

def_sources.label: Etymonline, s.v. hour (Latin horae ← Greek hōra)

etymology.origin: English rendering of Latin horae 'hours' (ult. Greek hōra 'season')
etymology.note: The runaway bestseller of the Middle Ages: prayers timed to the canonical hours, and the canvas for its greatest illumination.

flavor_snippets.html:
"Ours is to honour the martyr, tend the fallen, defend the innocent, and walk always in the light."
—*The Hospitaller's <mark>Book of Hours</mark>*
